worldview_shifts.to_view: The central government's policies can be actively harmful to the interests of farmers and states like Punjab.
worldview_shifts.trigger: The passing of the Farm Bills and the subsequent year-long protest by farmers.

pet_issues: Tackling malnutrition and anemia in women
Drug and alcohol abuse among youth in Punjab
Access to higher education for rural girls
vocabulary_quirks: Using English words like 'protocol', 'hemoglobin', and 'deficiency' in everyday Punjabi conversation.
"Koi gal ni" ('It's okay' or 'no problem'), used to de-escalate tension or reassure someone.
"Hona chahida hai" ('It should be done'), expressing a strong sense of moral or practical obligation.
aspirations: To qualify for a permanent government post as an ANM or health supervisor.
To see a generation of children in her village grow up fully vaccinated and without malnourishment.
To save enough money to build a 'pucca' house for her parents.
fears: A major health crisis in the village that she is unequipped to handle.
Her contract not being renewed, forcing her back into financial dependency.
Becoming alienated from her own community because of her education and modern views.

demographic.occupation: retired senior IAS officer (former Cabinet Secretariat)

biography.narrative_summary: I was born in the early sixties to a family that had moved to Delhi from Uttar Pradesh, seeking a better future. My father was in a modest government service, and for us, education was the only way up. The dream, instilled early, was the Indian Administrative Service. It was a singular focus, a marathon of study that culminated in my joining the 1989 batch at Mussoorie. My career began in the last days of the 'License Raj,' a system we were trained to uphold. Then, almost overnight, 1991 happened. We were the instruments of an epochal change, dismantling the very structures we knew. From district postings grappling with ground realities to the policy corridors of the Cabinet Secretariat, I have witnessed India's transformation from the inside. It has been a privilege to serve, to have a hand, however small, in the shaping of the nation. Now in retirement, I find myself reflecting on the journey. We have come far, but the challenges of maintaining institutional integrity and social cohesion are greater than ever. I try to contribute where I can, mostly by writing and mentoring, hoping the foundational ideals of the Republic endure.

eras.name: Delhi Childhood
eras.description: Growing up in a government servants' colony. A life focused on academics and discipline. Saw Delhi transform from a quieter city to a bustling metropolis.

eras.name: University & UPSC
eras.place: Delhi University & Jawaharlal Nehru University, Delhi
eras.description: Studied History at St. Stephen's College, followed by a Master's at JNU. An intellectually vibrant period, followed by years of intense, isolated preparation for the Civil Services Examination.

eras.name: Field Postings & The New India
eras.place: LBSNAA, Mussoorie; various districts in Uttar Pradesh and Bihar cadres
eras.description: The formative years as an officer. From the academy to managing districts as a DM. Directly implementing the new economic policies post-1991 and confronting the complexities of caste, religion, and politics on the ground.

eras.name: Secretariat Years & Retirement
eras.description: Deputation to the central government, rising through ministries to a senior role in the Cabinet Secretariat. Involved in high-level policy formulation and inter-ministerial coordination. Transitioned to a quiet, retired life in South Delhi.

formative_experiences.event: Successfully clearing the UPSC Civil Services Examination in my final attempt.
formative_experiences.impact: This was the single most defining event, transforming my social and economic standing and setting the entire course for my professional and personal life. It was a validation of years of sacrifice.

formative_experiences.event: The imposition of the National Emergency.
formative_experiences.impact: As a young teenager, I witnessed the fragility of democratic norms and the immense, unchecked power of the state. It gave me a lifelong lesson in the importance of constitutional checks and balances.

formative_experiences.event: The announcement of the 1991 economic reforms while serving as a young SDM.
formative_experiences.impact: It was a seismic shift. My training was for a state-controlled economy. We had to unlearn and adapt rapidly. It taught me that pragmatism must always trump ideology in governance.

formative_experiences.event: Managing the response to a major flood that devastated half my district.
formative_experiences.impact: Coordinating relief and rescue with limited resources was a crucible. It taught me crisis management, the importance of the human element in administration, and a deep respect for the resilience of ordinary people.

cultural_influences.title: Bharat Ek Khoj
cultural_influences.creator: Shyam Benegal
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: Based on Nehru's book, it was a profound visual journey through Indian history that reinforced a pluralistic, syncretic idea of India just as I was about to enter service.

cultural_influences.title: The Indian Express
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: A daily habit for over 40 years. Its investigative journalism and critical editorials, especially during the Emergency, shaped my belief in the necessity of a free press.

cultural_influences.title: Ardh Satya
cultural_influences.creator: Govind Nihalani
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: A stark, sobering look at the compromises and pressures within the system. It was a cautionary tale I carried with me throughout my career.

cultural_influences.title: Ae Mere Pyare Watan
cultural_influences.creator: Manna Dey (singer), Prem Dhawan (lyricist)
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: From the film 'Kabuliwala'. It evokes a deep, quiet patriotism, a love for the land and its people that is more profound than loud nationalism.

cultural_influences.title: Tryst with Destiny
cultural_influences.creator: Jawaharlal Nehru
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: Studied in school, its idealism and sense of historical purpose became a touchstone for what public service ought to mean.

cultural_influences.title: Live performances by Harivansh Rai Bachchan
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: My father loved poetry and would take me to these events. Bachchan's 'Madhushala' taught me about philosophy and life's complexities through accessible, beautiful Hindi.

top_5_books.title: The Discovery of India
top_5_books.creator: Jawaharlal Nehru
top_5_books.why_it_mattered: It shaped my foundational understanding of India as a complex, continuous civilization. It provided the 'idea of India' that I felt I was serving.

top_5_books.title: Raag Darbari
top_5_books.creator: Shrilal Shukla
top_5_books.why_it_mattered: Read during my first district posting. It was a brilliant, satirical mirror to the rural administration and politics I was witnessing. It provided a much-needed dose of cynical wisdom.

top_5_books.title: The Argumentative Indian
top_5_books.creator: Amartya Sen
top_5_books.why_it_mattered: It articulated so well the tradition of public debate and heterodoxy in India. It reinforced my belief in deliberation and dialogue as essential tools of governance.

top_5_books.title: India After Gandhi
top_5_books.creator: Ramachandra Guha
top_5_books.why_it_mattered: As a practitioner who lived through much of this history, Guha's comprehensive narrative provided context and perspective on the events I had experienced from my specific vantage point.

top_5_books.title: Meditations
top_5_books.creator: Marcus Aurelius
top_5_books.why_it_mattered: A private companion during stressful mid-career postings. The stoic philosophy of focusing on what one can control was immensely practical for navigating the pressures of my job.

mentors.teaching: He taught me the difference between the letter of the law and the spirit of service, and how to balance bureaucratic procedure with human compassion. He called it 'reading the file and reading the face'.
He instilled a habit of critical inquiry and looking for primary sources, not just accepting received wisdom. He taught me how to construct an argument, a skill essential for writing policy notes.

worldview_shifts.from_view: Idealistic belief that policy, correctly designed in the capital, will solve problems on the ground.
worldview_shifts.to_view: Pragmatic understanding that implementation is everything and is shaped by complex local realities that policy-makers often ignore.
worldview_shifts.trigger: First year as an SDM, seeing well-intentioned poverty alleviation schemes fail due to local politics and corruption.

worldview_shifts.from_view: The state is the primary and best agent of development and change.
worldview_shifts.to_view: The state's role is to be an effective facilitator and regulator for private enterprise and civil society, not the sole provider.
worldview_shifts.trigger: Witnessing the dynamism and job creation unleashed by the IT boom in the late 90s, which happened largely outside government control.

vocabulary_quirks: "Let us examine the matter in its totality."
"As per the extant procedures..."
"One must distinguish between the normative and the positive."
aspirations: To write a definitive book on the evolution of Indian economic policy from an insider's perspective.
To see my grandchildren grow up in a stable, prosperous, and tolerant India.
To establish a small foundation for mentoring underprivileged civil service aspirants.
fears: The erosion of institutional autonomy and integrity.
A rise in anti-intellectualism and the dismissal of expert opinion.
That social polarization will undo decades of economic progress.

demographic.occupation: senior fellow at a Pune-based public-policy think tank

biography.narrative_summary: I was born and raised in Pune, in a family that valued education above all else. My father used to say, 'Saraswati comes before Lakshmi,' and that was the principle of our home. This city, with its libraries and lecture halls, was my world. I pursued economics at the Gokhale Institute, fascinated by the architecture of a nation's finances. My career began just as India opened its economy in 1991, and I had a front-row seat to the most dramatic transformation in our modern history. The debates around the FRBM Act in the early 2000s were a formative period, sharpening my belief in fiscal prudence balanced with social imperatives. Now, as a senior fellow, my work is less about the heat of debate and more about the light of data. I try to understand the long-term consequences of our policy choices and guide younger researchers. I remain a firm believer in the power of reasoned argument and empirical evidence to solve our most complex problems, though I admit that belief is tested more frequently these days.

eras.name: Quiet Pune Childhood
eras.description: Growing up in a pre-liberalization Pune that was quieter, more academic. Family environment emphasized reading and discipline. Attended a well-regarded Marathi-medium school.

eras.name: University and Awakening
eras.description: Studied Economics at Fergusson College and then the Gokhale Institute of Politics and Economics. Deep immersion in theory while witnessing the limitations of the 'License Raj' economy firsthand.

eras.name: The Reform Years
eras.description: Early career as a policy researcher during the exciting post-1991 liberalization period. Actively participated in policy circles and debates, especially around fiscal consolidation and the FRBM Act.

eras.name: Senior Fellow and Mentor
eras.description: Established as a senior academic at a think tank. Focus shifted to long-term structural issues, writing, and mentoring the next generation of policy analysts. Greater engagement with issues of urban governance and fiscal federalism.

formative_experiences.event: Read Iravati Karve's 'Yuganta' for the first time, borrowed from her father's library.
formative_experiences.impact: It was the first time I saw revered traditions being subjected to rational, anthropological analysis. It gave me the intellectual permission to question and to seek evidence, which became the foundation of my academic approach.

formative_experiences.event: Won a prestigious inter-university debate on economic policy, arguing for market-oriented reforms.
formative_experiences.impact: The victory, and the rigour required to prepare for it, solidified my desire to pursue a career in public policy and economics, rather than a more conventional path.

formative_experiences.event: Was invited to be part of a non-governmental consultation group during the drafting of the FRBM Act rules.
formative_experiences.impact: This was my first direct, tangible experience of academic work influencing national policy. It was a powerful lesson in the practical application of my research and the art of political compromise.

cultural_influences.title: The Indian Express
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: Its strong editorial line and investigative journalism, especially during The Emergency, shaped my understanding of the press's role in a democracy.

cultural_influences.title: Sinhasan
cultural_influences.creator: Jabbar Patel
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: A Marathi political drama that offered a cynical but realistic look at power. It was a crucial early lesson in political economy, showing how policy ideals get compromised by political reality.

cultural_influences.title: Malgudi Days
cultural_influences.creator: Shankar Nag
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: Offered a gentle, humane vision of India that was a counterpoint to the often-strident political and economic debates I was immersed in. It was a reminder of the ordinary lives that policy is meant to serve.

cultural_influences.title: Budget Speeches of Manmohan Singh (1991) and P. Chidambaram (1997)
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: These were not just fiscal statements; they were articulations of a new idea of India. I studied them like texts, understanding how economic philosophy is translated into policy and communicated to the public.

cultural_influences.title: Batatyachi Chal
cultural_influences.creator: P. L. Deshpande
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: The quintessential Pune middle-class cultural text. Its humour and keen observations grounded me in my own culture and taught me the importance of a light touch, even when dealing with serious subjects.

cultural_influences.title: Ghashiram Kotwal
cultural_influences.creator: Vijay Tendulkar
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: Encountered this play in college. A powerful critique of power and social decay. It inoculated me against taking historical or political narratives at face value.

top_5_books.title: Yuganta: The End of an Epoch
top_5_books.creator: Iravati Karve
top_5_books.why_it_mattered: It taught me to apply an analytical, evidence-based lens to even the most sacred narratives. Foundational to my intellectual identity.

top_5_books.title: Development as Freedom
top_5_books.creator: Amartya Sen
top_5_books.why_it_mattered: Provided the ethical and philosophical framework for my belief in liberal economics. It connected economic policy to the higher goal of human flourishing.

top_5_books.title: We the People
top_5_books.creator: Nani Palkhivala
top_5_books.why_it_mattered: His clear defense of constitutional principles and economic liberty was a huge influence in my formative years. His analysis of the Union Budget was an annual event.

top_5_books.title: Kosala
top_5_books.creator: Bhalchandra Nemade
top_5_books.why_it_mattered: This novel's existentialist, anti-establishment tone captured the restlessness of my generation and broke from traditional Marathi literary forms. It represents an intellectual modernism.

top_5_books.title: The Sleepwalkers: How Europe Went to War in 1914
top_5_books.creator: Christopher Clark
top_5_books.why_it_mattered: Read much later in life, it was a profound reminder of how complex systems can fail and how rational actors can collectively produce irrational outcomes. It tempered my policy optimism with a dose of historical humility.

mentors.relationship: Doctoral guide at the Gokhale Institute
mentors.teaching: He taught me the meaning of academic rigour. He would tear apart my drafts, forcing me to defend every assumption with data and theory. It was brutal but necessary.

mentors.relationship: A senior journalist I met during policy consultations in Delhi
mentors.teaching: She taught me how to communicate complex economic ideas to a non-specialist audience without dumbing them down. 'If you can't explain it to me, you don't understand it yourself,' she'd say.

worldview_shifts.from_view: A technocratic belief that the right economic models and policies would automatically lead to broad-based development.
worldview_shifts.to_view: An understanding that political economy, institutional capacity, and social structures are often more significant barriers to development than policy design.
worldview_shifts.trigger: Observing the uneven and often inequitable impact of the first decade of economic reforms, and seeing well-designed state-level projects fail due to political interference or bureaucratic apathy.

vocabulary_quirks: "Let's look at the data."
"What is the counterfactual here?"
"That's a necessary but not sufficient condition."
aspirations: To write a seminal book on India's fiscal history from 1991-2021.
To see the Right to Information Act used more effectively for government accountability.
To mentor a new generation of female public policy scholars.
fears: A systemic erosion of democratic institutions and academic freedom.
The return of competitive populism leading to fiscal irresponsibility.
That public discourse will become so polarized that genuine, evidence-based debate becomes impossible.

biography.narrative_summary: I am from a small village in Saran. For as long as I can remember, the goal was to get a 'sarkari naukri'. It was the only respectable way out. After my graduation from Patna, I, like thousands of others, went to Delhi with the dream of clearing the UPSC civil services exam. I gave it my everything for four years, living in a small room in Mukherjee Nagar, reading 12 hours a day, sacrificing everything. But it was not to be. Returning home felt like a defeat. But then I saw the struggles of the farmers here, my own people, dealing with middlemen and unpredictable markets. The same analytical skills I had honed for the exam, I realised, could be used to understand supply chains and government policies. We started a small Farmer-Producer Organisation. It was difficult, but now, when I see the farmers getting a better price, a sense of collective strength building, it feels more real than that LBSNAA dream ever did. I didn't become an officer of the state, but I am serving my community directly. Perhaps this is its own kind of service.

eras.name: Childhood in Saran
eras.description: Attended the local government school. Saw the difficulties of farming firsthand – debt, monsoon dependency, lack of market access. The dream of a stable government job was instilled early on.

eras.name: Graduation and UPSC Beckoning
eras.description: Studied for a B.A. in History. Lived in a shared lodge, got swept up in the competitive exam culture. Started initial preparations for civil services.

eras.name: The Delhi Attempt
eras.description: Full-time, intensive UPSC preparation. Multiple attempts at the exam, facing failure and immense pressure. Witnessed the farmers' protests at the Delhi border, which deeply affected my perspective on agricultural policy.

eras.name: Return and a New Path
eras.description: Returned home after failing to clear UPSC. A period of disillusionment followed by a new sense of purpose in organizing local farmers to form an FPO. Applying academic knowledge to practical problems.

formative_experiences.event: Witnessing the state-wide impact and relief efforts following the Kosi river flood. Our area was not the worst hit, but the news and the stories of devastation were everywhere.
formative_experiences.impact: It was the first time I understood the scale of rural vulnerability and the critical role of state administration in people's lives. It solidified my desire for a government job to be 'part of the solution'.

formative_experiences.event: Failing to clear the UPSC Civil Services Preliminary exam for the second time by a very narrow margin.
formative_experiences.impact: A severe crisis of confidence. It forced a re-evaluation of my abilities and the very definition of 'success' I had held for years.

formative_experiences.event: Observing the large-scale farmers' protests against the new farm laws near the Delhi border.
formative_experiences.impact: The theoretical concepts from my GS papers—MSP, APMC, contract farming—became starkly real. I saw the power of collective bargaining and felt a disconnect between policy-making in Delhi and the 'zameeni hakikat' (ground reality).

formative_experiences.event: Successfully negotiating the FPO's first major bulk contract with a food processing company, bypassing local middlemen entirely.
formative_experiences.impact: A powerful moment of validation. It proved that my new path was viable and could create tangible economic benefits for my community. It felt more significant than clearing any exam.

cultural_influences.title: The Hindu
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: It was my bible during UPSC preparation. It taught me how to analyze issues from multiple perspectives, but its language and focus also felt very distant from my village.

cultural_influences.title: Swades
cultural_influences.creator: Ashutosh Gowariker
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: The story of an educated person returning to their village to solve local problems resonated deeply, first as an inspiration and later as an almost literal life path.

cultural_influences.title: Study IQ IAS
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: Made complex topics in economics and polity accessible. It was a lifeline in Delhi, a teacher that was always available.

cultural_influences.title: Tryst with Destiny
cultural_influences.creator: Jawaharlal Nehru
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: Studied for Modern History. The idealism and the sense of building a nation from scratch was very moving. It connected my small ambitions to a larger national project.

cultural_influences.title: Ramcharitmanas
cultural_influences.creator: Tulsidas
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: Heard it recited since childhood. It formed my basic moral and cultural framework – ideas of dharma, duty, and perseverance.

cultural_influences.title: Lollipop Lagelu
cultural_influences.creator: Pawan Singh
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: It's not profound, but it's the sound of home. In the stressful environment of Delhi, listening to Bhojpuri songs was a way to connect with my roots and relax.

top_5_books.title: India After Gandhi: The History of the World's Largest Democracy
top_5_books.creator: Ramachandra Guha
top_5_books.why_it_mattered: Essential for UPSC. It gave me a framework to understand the complexities of modern India, beyond headlines.

top_5_books.title: Annihilation of Caste
top_5_books.creator: B. R. Ambedkar
top_5_books.why_it_mattered: Reading Ambedkar was a revelation. It gave me the language and theory to understand the structural realities of caste I had always observed but couldn't articulate.

top_5_books.title: Godan
top_5_books.creator: Munshi Premchand
top_5_books.why_it_mattered: The story of Hori felt like the story of my own ancestors. It's the most powerful depiction of agrarian crisis and farmer debt I have ever read.

top_5_books.title: Everybody Loves a Good Drought
top_5_books.creator: P. Sainath
top_5_books.why_it_mattered: I read this after returning from Delhi. It connected the dots between policy and rural distress. It shaped the problem-solving approach I now take with the FPO.

top_5_books.title: Certificate Physical and Human Geography
top_5_books.creator: Goh Cheng Leong
top_5_books.why_it_mattered: A textbook, yes, but it fundamentally changed how I see the land. Understanding monsoon patterns, soil types, and climate is no longer academic; it's essential for my work.

mentors.teaching: He guided me on 'how to study' for UPSC, which books to read, and how to manage time. He was my first real guide into that world.
A supportive BDO who helped me and other farmers navigate the bureaucracy of registering our FPO. He showed that the 'system' can work for people if you know how to engage with it.

worldview_shifts.from_view: The only way to create large-scale change and earn respect is by becoming an IAS officer and wielding state power.
worldview_shifts.to_view: Meaningful, sustainable change can be built from the ground up through community organization and economic empowerment, independent of the state.
worldview_shifts.trigger: Failing the civil services exam multiple times, combined with witnessing the farmers' protests and later seeing the practical success of a local cooperative.

vocabulary_quirks: Using phrases like 'fiscal consolidation' or 'socio-economic indicators' in Bhojpuri/Hindi conversation.
Frequently saying 'Zameeni hakikat yeh hai...' (The ground reality is...)
Referring to problems as needing a 'holistic approach'.
aspirations: To make our FPO a self-sustaining, profitable model for the entire state.
To ensure my family is never again in a precarious financial position.
Maybe write for a newspaper about agricultural issues someday.
fears: A sudden change in government policy making our FPO unviable.
That the collective spirit of the farmers will fracture due to local politics.
Being unable to keep up with new agricultural technologies and market trends.

biography.narrative_summary: My life has followed the rhythm of silk. From the time I was a boy, my hands have known the feel of mulberry leaves and the tension of the thread on the loom. I am a farmer first, then a weaver. My day starts in the fields, caring for the plants that feed the worms. This is a delicate business; 'Reshme krishi andre makkalna saakid ಹಾಗೆ' – sericulture is like raising children. We are part of a cooperative here, which helps us small people stand together. Every week, I travel to the Ramanagara mandi. It is a noisy, chaotic place, but it is where our hard work turns into money for the family. I am proud to weave Mysore silk, a craft passed down from my father. But it is a hard life. The prices change like the weather, and I worry if my children will want to continue this work. Still, there is a satisfaction in creating something beautiful from the earth. When I see a fine silk saree, I see the whole year's labour in its sheen.

eras.name: Apprenticeship and Schooling
eras.description: Grew up in a weaving household, learning the basics of sericulture and weaving from my father after school. Attended the local government school until SSLC.

eras.name: Full-time Weaver and Young Family Man
eras.description: Left school to work full-time. Got married and started a family. Joined the local weavers' cooperative, which was a major step towards stability.

eras.name: Navigating a Changing Market
eras.description: Became an experienced artisan and a regular at the Ramanagara silk market. Faced challenges from economic liberalization and competition from power looms, reinforcing the importance of the cooperative.

eras.name: Elder of the Craft
eras.description: Continuing the craft while mentoring younger weavers. Concerned about the future of handloom and the next generation's interest. Adapted to new realities like demonetisation and the pandemic.

formative_experiences.event: First time successfully managing a small batch of silkworms from egg to cocoon on my own.
formative_experiences.impact: Instilled a deep sense of responsibility, pride, and connection to the craft of sericulture.

formative_experiences.event: Joined the local handloom weavers' cooperative after a year of fluctuating, exploitative prices from middlemen.
formative_experiences.impact: Solidified a belief in collective action and community support over individual struggle. Provided economic stability.

formative_experiences.event: Travelled to the Ramanagara market for the first time by myself and negotiated a good price for our cooperative's cocoons.
formative_experiences.impact: A major rite of passage that built confidence and gave me a wider perspective on the silk industry beyond my village.

formative_experiences.event: Heard the news of the Babri Masjid demolition on the radio, leading to communal tensions felt even in our relatively peaceful region.
formative_experiences.impact: Created a lasting sense of vulnerability and reinforced the importance of immediate community and faith as a source of security.

cultural_influences.title: Bangarada Manushya
cultural_influences.creator: Siddalingaiah (Director)
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: The story of a man returning to his village to make it prosper through agriculture was very inspiring. It made farming feel like noble work.

cultural_influences.title: Prajavani
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: My main source of news about the state, government schemes for farmers, and market prices. Reading it is a daily ritual.

cultural_influences.title: Malgudi Days
cultural_influences.creator: Shankar Nag (Director)
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: It was set in a world that felt familiar, even if it was in Hindi. The simple stories of ordinary people felt very real and comforting.

cultural_influences.title: The Qur'an
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: Heard and recited since childhood. It is the foundation of my faith and moral compass. The Friday khutba connects its teachings to our daily lives.

cultural_influences.title: Kannada Bhavageethegalu on All India Radio
cultural_influences.creator: Various artists like C. Ashwath
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: The background music to my evenings working on the loom. The poetry about nature, life, and philosophy is calming.

cultural_influences.title: Weekly Friday Khutba
cultural_influences.creator: Local Imam
cultural_influences.why_it_mattered: A source of spiritual guidance and community connection, explaining religious principles in a way that applies to our lives as farmers and artisans.

top_5_books.title: The Qur'an
top_5_books.why_it_mattered: The central text of my life. I learned to read the Arabic script for it. Its guidance is what I turn to for big questions.

top_5_books.title: Prajavani
top_5_books.why_it_mattered: Though not a book, I have read this more than any book. It keeps me connected to the world beyond my village.

top_5_books.title: Kannada Nudi
top_5_books.creator: Govt of Karnataka
top_5_books.why_it_mattered: My 10th standard Kannada textbook. It was my last formal contact with literature. I still remember some of the poems by Kuvempu and Bendre.

top_5_books.title: Reshme Krishi Kaipidi
top_5_books.creator: Karnataka State Sericulture Department
top_5_books.why_it_mattered: A government handbook on modern sericulture practices. It's a practical guide that helped us improve our cocoon yield.

top_5_books.title: Devaraj Urs's speeches on land reform
top_5_books.creator: D. Devaraj Urs
top_5_books.why_it_mattered: Heard on the radio as a boy. He was a Chief Minister who spoke for the poor and backward classes. It gave us a sense that the government could be on our side.

mentors.teaching: Taught me the entire craft of sericulture and weaving, not as a job but as a family tradition and responsibility.
An elder who guided us on how to negotiate at the market, the importance of saving, and how to navigate government schemes.

worldview_shifts.from_view: My family's success or failure depends only on our own hard work.
worldview_shifts.to_view: Our success is tied to our community; we must work together to bargain with larger forces.
worldview_shifts.trigger: A year of severe exploitation by middlemen, which was resolved only after joining the weavers' cooperative.

pet_issues: The decline of traditional crafts among the youth
vocabulary_quirks: "Reshme krishi andre makkalna saakid ಹಾಗೆ" (Sericulture is like raising children)
Frequent use of "Inshallah" when speaking of future plans
Referring to peers and colleagues as "bhaijaan"
aspirations: To see my children get a good education and stable jobs, even if outside weaving.
To pay off my loans from the cooperative society.
To own a slightly more modern, efficient loom.
fears: A disease wiping out the silkworm stock
Falling ill and being unable to work the loom
The government withdrawing subsidies for weavers
